content.text: Serverless computing is a cloud execution model where you write and deploy code without managing any servers. The cloud provider allocates compute resources on demand, scales to zero when your code is not running, and charges you only for the execution time consumed — measured in milliseconds and memory-seconds.

"Serverless" is a misleading name: servers still exist. What disappears is the operational burden of provisioning, patching, capacity-planning, and load-balancing those servers. That work shifts entirely to the cloud provider.
Functions as a Service (FaaS) is the primary serverless primitive: you deploy a function that responds to a trigger, runs to completion, and exits. Azure Functions is Microsoft's FaaS offering.

The diagram below shows the reference architecture: clients call APIs through Azure API Management, which routes to Azure Functions. Functions connect to data stores and messaging services using managed identity (no stored credentials).

attrs.alt: Azure serverless architecture: Client → API Management → Azure Functions (HTTP, Timer, Queue, Event Grid, Blob triggers) → Cosmos DB, Azure SQL, Service Bus, Blob Storage, Key Vault
attrs.title: Azure Serverless Architecture

content.text: Every Azure Function has exactly one
— the event that causes the function to run. Functions also support
— declarative connections to input or output data sources that are configured in code rather than written as boilerplate.
New or modified blob in Storage
React to resource changes, IoT events
— fully serverless; scales to zero when idle; pay per execution and GB-second. Cold starts (50 ms – 3 s) occur when an instance wakes from zero. Best for irregular, spiky traffic.
— combines the pay-per-use billing of Consumption with pre-provisioned instances for cold-start elimination. Supports VNet integration and larger memory sizes (up to 4 GB).
— always-warm pre-warmed instances, VNet integration, larger SKUs (EP1–EP3), unlimited execution duration. Priced per vCore-hour. Right for latency-sensitive or compliance-heavy workloads.
— Functions run on a dedicated App Service Plan alongside web apps. No per-execution billing; cost is fixed per plan. Use when you already have underutilized App Service capacity.

import azure.functions as func
import logging
import json

app = func.FunctionApp()

@app.service_bus_queue_trigger(
    arg_name="msg",
    queue_name="orders",
    connection="SERVICE_BUS_CONNECTION"  # app setting name, not the value
)
@app.cosmos_db_output(
    arg_name="outputDoc",
    database_name="shop",
    container_name="processed_orders",
    connection="COSMOS_CONNECTION",
    create_if_not_exists=True
)
def process_order(msg: func.ServiceBusMessage, outputDoc: func.Out[func.Document]) -> None:
    order = json.loads(msg.get_body().decode())
    logging.info(f"Processing order {order['id']}")
    # ... business logic ...
    outputDoc.set(func.Document.from_dict({**order, "status": "processed"}))

# Create a function app on Consumption plan
az group create --name rg-functions --location eastus

az storage account create \
  --name stfunctions$RANDOM \
  --resource-group rg-functions \
  --sku Standard_LRS

az functionapp create \
  --resource-group rg-functions \
  --consumption-plan-location eastus \
  --runtime python \
  --runtime-version 3.11 \
  --functions-version 4 \
  --name my-func-app-$RANDOM \
  --storage-account <storage-account-name> \
  --os-type linux

# Assign managed identity so the function can reach Key Vault / Cosmos / etc.
az functionapp identity assign \
  --name my-func-app \
  --resource-group rg-functions

# Deploy code
func azure functionapp publish my-func-app
Standard Functions are stateless — each invocation starts fresh.
extends Azure Functions with an orchestration layer that checkpoints state to Azure Storage after each activity completes, so orchestrations survive function host restarts.
Other Durable patterns include: chaining (A → B → C), human-approval workflows (wait for external event), eternal loops (monitor pattern), and saga compensation (rollback on failure).
Azure Functions is the FaaS core, but the Azure serverless portfolio is wider:
— low-code workflow orchestration with 400+ connectors. Configure integration flows in a visual designer without writing code. Single-tenant (Standard) and multi-tenant (Consumption) modes.
— fully managed event routing service. Publishers push events; subscribers (Functions, Logic Apps, webhooks) receive them. Supports at-least-once delivery with retry.
— serverless container hosting with KEDA-based autoscaling (scale to zero). Runs any containerized workload; can host microservices with Dapr sidecar support.
— managed API gateway; sits in front of Functions to handle auth, rate limiting, caching, and request/response transformation.
On the Consumption plan, when no instance has run recently, the platform must provision a new instance, download your code, start the runtime, and invoke your function. This
latency is typically 200 ms – 3 s for Python functions and varies with package size.
Mitigation strategies, roughly in order of effectiveness:
Use Flex Consumption or Premium plan — keep at least one pre-warmed instance alive.
Minimize package size — remove unused dependencies; use slim base images for container-deployed functions.
Move heavy imports outside the function handler (module-level initialization runs once per instance).
Use a lightweight ping on a timer trigger every 5–10 minutes to keep instances warm (Consumption plan only; not cost-free).
Azure Functions can be assigned a managed identity, eliminating the need for connection strings in application settings. SDK clients that accept
pick up the identity automatically at runtime.

# No connection string needed — managed identity is used at runtime
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.cosmos import CosmosClient
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# Credential is resolved from the environment:
# local dev → az login / VS Code
# Azure     → managed identity
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Connect to Cosmos DB without a key
cosmos = CosmosClient(
    url="https://myaccount.documents.azure.com",
    credential=credential
)

# Fetch a secret from Key Vault without storing it in app settings
kv = SecretClient(vault_url="https://myvault.vault.azure.net", credential=credential)
connection_string = kv.get_secret("db-connection-string").value

# .github/workflows/deploy-functions.yml
name: Deploy Azure Functions
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt --target .python_packages/lib/site-packages

      - name: Azure login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Deploy to Azure Functions
        uses: azure/functions-action@v1
        with:
          app-name: my-func-app
          package: .
          scm-do-build-during-deployment: false
Azure Functions integrates with Application Insights automatically when you set
in app settings. Every execution is logged with duration, success/failure, and any custom telemetry you emit.

import logging
from opencensus.ext.azure.log_exporter import AzureLogHandler

# Standard logging automatically flows to Application Insights
logging.info("Processing started")
logging.warning("Retrying after transient error")
logging.error("Failed to write to Cosmos DB", exc_info=True)

# Custom metrics via Application Insights SDK
from applicationinsights import TelemetryClient
tc = TelemetryClient()  # reads APPINSIGHTS_INSTRUMENTATIONKEY from env
tc.track_metric("orders_processed", 1)
tc.flush()
Key Application Insights queries for Functions:

// Failed executions in the last hour
requests
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| where success == false
| summarize count() by name, resultCode
| order by count_ desc

// p99 duration by function name
requests
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| summarize percentile(duration, 99) by name
| order by percentile_duration_99 desc
Default timeout is 5 minutes (max 10 minutes on Consumption). Offload long work to Durable Functions or a background queue.
The same instance may serve multiple requests concurrently (depending on the plan). State must go in Cosmos DB, Redis, or Table Storage — not module-level variables.
Creating SDK clients on every invocation wastes latency and can exhaust connection pools. Initialize clients at module level (outside the function handler) so they are reused across warm invocations.
All secrets go in app settings or, better, in Key Vault references. The function accesses them through environment variables at runtime.
Not handling idempotency for queue triggers.
Service Bus and Storage Queue triggers guarantee at-least-once delivery. Your function must be idempotent — processing the same message twice must produce the same result.
Keep functions small and single-purpose. A function that does one thing is easier to test, scale, and debug.
Use managed identity for all service connections — no secrets in app settings, no rotation needed.
Enable Application Insights from day one. Debugging a production issue without telemetry is extremely difficult in a serverless environment.
Test locally with the Azure Functions Core Tools before deploying. The
command runs the local host and fires triggers from local storage emulators.
Use Durable Functions for any workflow that spans more than a few seconds or requires coordination across multiple activities.
Set concurrency limits on queue triggers to avoid overwhelming downstream dependencies:
Runs once a day; no server needed 23h/day
High-traffic API with steady load (>1000 req/s)
Premium plan works; compare cost vs App Service
Use Azure Machine Learning or GPU VMs

Think of serverless like hiring a freelance driver instead of buying a car. You only pay when they drive; you don't maintain, insure, or garage the vehicle. For occasional trips this is dramatically cheaper and simpler. For someone who commutes 100 km daily, a dedicated car becomes more cost-effective. Match the model to your traffic pattern.

Azure Functions Core Tools provide a local runtime that mirrors the production host. You can invoke HTTP functions with curl, simulate queue messages, and debug with a standard IDE debugger — all without deploying to Azure.

# Install Core Tools (macOS / Linux)
npm install -g azure-functions-core-tools@4 --unsafe-perm true

# Start the local host
func start

# The host prints each registered function and its URL:
# Functions:
#     hello: [GET,POST] http://localhost:7071/api/hello

# Invoke the HTTP function
curl http://localhost:7071/api/hello?name=Azure
# {"message": "Hello, Azure!"}

# Simulate a timer trigger fire (forces an immediate execution)
curl -X POST http://localhost:7071/admin/functions/daily_cleanup \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"input": null}'
Because the v2 Python programming model uses plain functions decorated with
, you can unit-test the handler directly by constructing a mock

# test_hello.py
import pytest
import azure.functions as func
from function_app import hello

def test_hello_with_name():
    req = func.HttpRequest(
        method='GET',
        body=b'',
        url='http://localhost:7071/api/hello',
        params={'name': 'Deiva'}
    )
    response = hello(req)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    import json
    body = json.loads(response.get_body())
    assert body['message'] == 'Hello, Deiva!'

def test_hello_default_name():
    req = func.HttpRequest(
        method='GET',
        body=b'',
        url='http://localhost:7071/api/hello',
        params={}
    )
    response = hello(req)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    body = json.loads(response.get_body())
    assert body['message'] == 'Hello, World!'
The Consumption plan pricing has two components: execution count and execution time (GB-seconds). The monthly free grant covers 1 million executions and 400,000 GB-seconds. Beyond that:
$0.000016 per GB-second. A function using 256 MB for 500 ms consumes 0.125 GB-seconds (0.256 GB × 0.5 s).
Example: 10 million executions per month, each consuming 256 MB for 200 ms:
GB-seconds: 10M × 0.256 GB × 0.2 s = 512,000 GB-s. Free grant covers 400,000 → billable 112,000 × $0.000016 =
Total: ~$3.60/month — significantly cheaper than the smallest App Service plan ($13.14/month for B1).

At very high volumes (> 50 million executions/month with steady load), the Premium plan's per-vCore-hour pricing often becomes cheaper because you avoid paying per-execution overhead and get consistent performance without cold starts.
Azure Functions HTTP triggers have three authentication levels:
— no key required. Suitable when the function is behind API Management which enforces its own auth.
— requires a function-level API key in the
query param. Lightweight caller auth; not a replacement for OAuth.
— requires the host master key. Reserved for management operations; never expose to end users.
For user-facing APIs, prefer placing the function behind Azure API Management and using Entra ID OAuth2 tokens validated by APIM — do not rely on function keys alone for end-user authentication.

On the Consumption plan, functions run in a shared multi-tenant network. For compliance scenarios requiring private connectivity to backend resources (Azure SQL, Redis, Cosmos DB), upgrade to the Premium or Flex Consumption plan and enable VNet integration. Outbound traffic routes through your VNet; you can then lock down backend resources to accept traffic only from your VNet subnet.
Serverless/FaaS means deploying code without managing servers; the platform handles provisioning, scaling, and patching.
Azure Functions supports HTTP, Timer, Queue, Blob, Event Grid, and Cosmos DB triggers; bindings handle data I/O declaratively.
Hosting plans: Consumption (pay-per-use, scales to zero), Flex Consumption (pre-warmed + pay-per-use), Premium (always-warm, VNet), App Service Plan (dedicated).
Durable Functions adds stateful orchestrations — fan-out/fan-in, chaining, human approval, and saga patterns — without writing coordination infrastructure.
Use managed identity for all service connections and Application Insights for telemetry from day one.
Best fit: event-driven, short-duration, variable-traffic workloads. Not for long-running jobs or workloads that need sub-10 ms cold-start latency at all times.
